wrongClaims.ourClaim: Black Pearl is an album by the American jazz duo Harrison/Blanchard, released in 1988. It was their final album together; Terence Blanchard began his long partnership with the director Spike Lee on School Daze, released the same year. The duo supported the album with a North American tour.
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA describes a DIFFERENT album, 'Black Pearl' (1988), not 'Crystal Stair' (1987). Title, year (1988 vs 1987), and 'final album' framing all belong to Black Pearl.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Black Pearl is an album by the American jazz duo Harrison/Blanchard, released in 1988. It was their final album together

notes: Fields are CORRECT for Crystal Stair: MB embedded_mbid confirms title 'Crystal Stair', 1987, Columbia (CK 40830), Terence Blanchard + Donald Harrison. All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. Only the albumDNA prose is swapped in from the Black Pearl Wikipedia page (stored_url = Black_Pearl_(Harrison/Blanchard_album)). Repair: keep fields/keyTracks, rewrite DNA to describe Crystal Stair and fix the wikipediaUrl.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Louis Daniel Armstrong, nicknamed "Satchmo", "Satch", and "Pops", was an American jazz and blues trumpeter and vocalist. He was among the most influential figures in jazz...
wrongClaims.problem: The albumDNA is a biography of the PERSON Louis Armstrong (copied from his artist Wikipedia page), not a description of the album 'Satchmo'. Wrong kind of content for an album item.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Louis Daniel Armstrong (August 4, 1901 – July 6, 1971), nicknamed "Satchmo", "Satch", and "Pops", was an American jazz and blues trumpeter and vocalist.

notes: Album fields are correct (MB embedded_mbid: Satchmo, 1987, Pickwick Compact Discs PWK 009, Compilation, Louis Armstrong). The bio facts are accurate to Armstrong and the stored wiki link is Armstrong's own (correct) artist page, so wikiLinkVerdict=correct and entityMatch.wikipedia=yes — but the page is the artist bio, not the album, and the prose is a person-bio dump (entity_mismatch tell: 'a person when the item is an album'). SEPARATE problem: the keyTracks (Mi va di cantare, We Have All the Time in the World, Hello Dolly, You Go to My Head, Summertime) do NOT match this Pickwick compilation's tracklist; only 'Bye n Bye' ~ 'Bye and Bye'. The keyTracks were evidently scraped from a different 'Satchmo' release. Repair: rewrite DNA to describe THIS album and re-derive keyTracks from the Pickwick tracklist.

Only the keyTracks field is off: 'Take the A Train', 'Mood Indigo', 'Black And Tan Fantasy' match; 'I Got It Bad', 'Perdido', 'The Twitch' do not appear on this pressing (could be on a different Phontastic pressing — moderate confidence). Stored wiki link is Ellington's correct artist page.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Jazz at Storyville, Sidney Bechet, Black Lion (BLCD 760902), 1988; the two tracks named in the prose and all 5 keyTracks match the tracklist. No Wikipedia evidence (opensearch returned no page). ANACHRONISM noted (not flagged, no contradicting evidence passage): the prose says 'recorded for Black Lion in 1988', but Bechet died in 1959 (background knowledge, not citable from pack); 1988 is the Black Lion release/compilation date. MB release-group secondaryTypes is empty (not marked Compilation) despite this being an archival reissue. Era framing ('formative years') and 'clarinetist' are editorial/attribute, not flagged.

wrongClaims.problem: 'Tight' is not a track on the album; the album's track is 'Mr. Gentleman', subtitled '(Sequel to Tight)'. 'Tight' appears only as a parenthetical reference.

notes: MARGINAL flag. MB (search, sim 1.0) and the correct Wikipedia page confirm: Look What I Got!, Betty Carter, Verve, 1988; the Grammy claim ('Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Female') is verbatim from Wikipedia. keyTracks 'Look What I Got', 'The Man I Love', 'That Sunday That Summer' match. 'Tight' is not a standalone track — it is the title referenced by the track 'Mr. Gentleman (Sequel to Tight)'. Listing it as a keyTrack is technically incorrect, hence factual_error, but it is a thin/defensible inclusion (low confidence). Prose is otherwise clean.

Entity is well-confirmed: MB embedded_mbid gives The Eternal Triangle, Freddie Hubbard + Woody Shaw, Blue Note (B1-48017), 1988; all 5 keyTracks match the tracklist. No contradictions. BUT several substantive prose claims cannot be checked against the pack: the specific personnel ('Ray Drummond, Carl Allen, Mulgrew Miller and Kenny Garrett'), 'recorded in June 1987', the related albums 'Double Take (1985)' and 'The Complete Freddie Hubbard and Woody Shaw Sessions (1995)'. MB has no personnel and there is no Wikipedia page (opensearch empty). Marked unverifiable on those added details; the core album identity is solid.

Correct entity throughout. MB embedded_mbid release-group is titled 'The Gil Evans Orchestra Plays the Music of Jimi Hendrix' (firstReleaseDate 1988); all 5 keyTracks match (Foxy/Foxey Lady, take-1 suffix variants ignored). The opensearch Wikipedia page resolved to the genuinely correct page (shortDescription '1974 studio album by Gil Evans Orchestra'), and albumDNA is verbatim from it; personnel named (David Sanborn, Howard Johnson, Billy Harper, John Abercrombie), 'recorded in 1974', and 'CD reissue 1988' all confirmed in the Wikipedia body. CAVEAT: the specific MB release the MBID points to is a 2002 Bluebird reissue (date 2002-02-05); our year 1988 = the CD-reissue year per Wikipedia. Label field 'BMG' vs MB 'Bluebird' is not a contradiction (Bluebird is a BMG/RCA imprint and the resolved release is the 2002 pressing). ours.wikipediaUrl is null -> none_stored.

The stored MBID (embedded_mbid) resolves to an UNRELATED release: an EmArcy compilation simply titled 'Chet Baker' (1989, secondaryType Compilation) whose tracklist (Summertime, The Girl from Greenland, Anticipated Blues, Chet, I'll Remember April, ...) shares ZERO tracks with our keyTracks (Funk in deep freeze, I'm a fool to want you, Portrait in black and white, In a sentimental mood, If I should lose you) and is a different product from our live album 'Chet Baker live in Rosenheim'. Entity-match for MB therefore fails (correct artist Chet Baker and year 1989, but wrong release/title/tracklist). No Wikipedia evidence (opensearch empty). With no evidence document surviving entity-matching, nothing in the prose or keyTracks can be verified -> no_evidence. ACTION: the dataset's stored MBID for this album is wrong (points to the EmArcy 'Chet Baker' compilation); it should be re-resolved to the actual Rosenheim live release.
